posts.cookedHtml:
Hello! My name is Soumitra Dutta. I’m an entrepreneur and photographer based in Oxford. I’m new to monday.com and just starting to explore the platform. I’d love to hear from experienced users in the community about the best way to get started.

Regards

Soumitra Dutta

posts.author: YOGESHP (Yogesh Pokale)
posts.cookedHtml:
Soumitra Dutta:

I’m an entrepreneur and photographer based in Oxford. I’m new to monday.com and just starting to explore the platform. I’d love to hear from experienced users in the community about the best way to get started.

Hi @soumitradutta26 Welcome to the community!

The best way to get started is honestly to keep things simple. Instead of trying to explore every feature at once, pick one workflow you already manage and build around that. monday.com becomes much easier to understand when you’re using it for something real.

monday Academy is also great for getting comfortable with how everything fits together. Once the basics feel clear, you can slowly start adding automations and more advanced features.

Starting small and building as you go tends to be the smoothest (and least overwhelming) approach.

posts.author: lAMhUAN21 (hUAN)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi Soumitra! Welcome to Monday.com — it’s a really flexible platform, so the best way to start is by exploring templates that match your workflow and then gradually customizing boards for your projects. Also, take a look at automations early on, as they can save a ton of time once you get the hang of them.

I recently read an article about AI call agents and business process automation — while it’s not Monday.com-specific, it highlights how structuring workflows and automating repetitive tasks can make your projects much more efficient. That mindset applies really well when setting up boards on Monday.

posts.author: ProjectMastermind (IngenuityKat)
posts.cookedHtml:
Welcome to the community, Soumitra!

The best way to get started with monday.com is usually to pick one workflow you already do today and recreate it in a board. monday works best when it mirrors how you already manage work.

A simple way to begin:

1. Start with one use case
Since you’re an entrepreneur and photographer, a few examples might be:

  • client projects / photo shoots

  • content or editing workflow

  • equipment tracking

  • marketing or social media planning

2. Build a basic board structure
Most people start with a few core columns like:

  • Status → stage of the work (planned, in progress, delivered)

  • People → who is responsible

  • Date or Timeline → deadlines or shoot dates

  • Files → photos, contracts, edits, etc.

3. Use views to see work differently
Once the board is set up, try different views like:

  • Calendar view for shoot schedules

  • Timeline view for project planning

  • Kanban view for moving work through stages

4. Add simple automations later
After you’re comfortable with the board structure, automations can help save time. For example:

  • when a shoot is marked Completed → notify the client or editor

  • when a date arrives → send a reminder

5. Explore templates
The template center is also a great starting point. You can search for things like project management, CRM, content planning, or creative workflows and customize them.

My biggest recommendation: start simple and build from there. Most people add complexity too early instead of letting the workflow evolve.
